title: Coffee Break

story.location: A neighborhood café beside Mercado de San Miguel
story.scene: Carlos carries the market bag into a quiet café. You sit on the terrace, choose what you genuinely prefer to drink, and order a drink and snack from the server.
story.mission: Today you’ll order a drink and snack politely and make one simple change.
story.continuity: The lesson begins moments after Lesson 5. Carlos briefly acknowledges the learner’s market choice, then turns naturally toward the café order.

emotionalArc.startsWith: The learner arrives carrying the energy of the busy market.
emotionalArc.endsWith: The learner feels comfortable enough to order slowly, clearly, and according to their own preference.
emotionalArc.earnedBy: Choosing a real drink preference and completing a warm café exchange without Carlos ordering for them.

apples.spanish: Mi madre está muy contenta con las manzanas.
apples.english: My mother is very happy with the apples.

bread.spanish: El pan fue perfecto para el almuerzo.
bread.english: The bread was perfect for lunch.

cheese.spanish: A mi familia le encanta el queso que elegiste.
cheese.english: My family loves the cheese you chose.

coffee.spanish: Elegiste café en el mercado. Sabía que te gustaría este lugar.
coffee.english: You picked coffee at the market. I knew you’d like this place.

flowers.spanish: Mi madre está muy contenta con las flores.
flowers.english: My mother is very happy with the flowers.

tomatoes.spanish: Los tomates fueron perfectos para el almuerzo.
tomatoes.english: The tomatoes were perfect for lunch.

microCliffhanger.speaker: Carlos
microCliffhanger.spanish: Después del café, quiero enseñarte una plaza muy bonita. Podemos ir caminando.
microCliffhanger.english: After coffee, I want to show you a beautiful plaza. We can walk there.

carlosIntroduction.title: Order like you belong here
carlosIntroduction.message: After that busy market, we deserve a break. This café makes my favorite café con leche. What do you prefer—coffee, tea, or orange juice? Today, you’re ordering for yourself.

canDo: Order one drink and one snack with quisiera.
Add a simple change with a familiar café chunk.
Respond to ¿Algo más? and close politely.
objectives: Choose a drink based on a genuine personal preference.
Use quisiera to make a polite café order.
Ask for one simple addition or removal.
Complete a short exchange with a server.

recycledLanguage.use: Open and close the café exchange warmly.
Understand when the café stop happens and what comes next.
Recognize familiar products and close another real transaction.

essentialPhrases.usage: Ask someone to choose between real options.
essentialPhrases.exampleEnglish: What do you prefer, coffee or tea?

essentialPhrases.usage: Make a polite order at a café.
essentialPhrases.exampleEnglish: I would like a coffee, please.

essentialPhrases.usage: Introduce your own part of a shared order.
Add milk as one simple change.
essentialPhrases.exampleSpanish: Un café con leche, por favor.

essentialPhrases.usage: Remove sugar as one simple change.
essentialPhrases.exampleSpanish: Un té sin azúcar, por favor.

essentialPhrases.usage: Recognize a very common service question.
Ask for the bill when you are ready to leave.

learnerChoices.objective: State a genuine drink preference and use it in a polite order.
learnerChoices.promptEnglish: What do you prefer: coffee, tea, or juice?

options.label: Coffee
options.carlosSpanish: Buena elección. Yo también prefiero el café con leche.
options.carlosEnglish: Good choice. I prefer coffee with milk too.
options.modelOrder: Quisiera un café con leche, por favor.
options.modelOrderEnglish: I would like a coffee with milk, please.

options.label: Tea
options.carlosSpanish: Perfecto. Aquí preparan un té muy bueno.
options.carlosEnglish: Perfect. They make very good tea here.
options.memoryCallback: Tea is your café choice. I’ll remember that.
options.modelOrder: Quisiera un té sin azúcar, por favor.
options.modelOrderEnglish: I would like a tea without sugar, please.

options.label: Orange Juice
options.carlosSpanish: Buena idea. Un zumo fresco viene perfecto después del mercado.
options.carlosEnglish: Good idea. Fresh juice is perfect after the market.
options.memoryCallback: Orange juice after the market—good choice. I’ll remember that.
options.modelOrder: Quisiera un zumo de naranja pequeño, por favor.
options.modelOrderEnglish: I would like a small orange juice, please.

learnerChoices.rejoin: The learner orders the selected drink, adds one snack, responds to ¿Algo más?, and asks for the bill.

vocabulary.tip: A cafetería serves coffee, drinks, and simple food.
vocabulary.exampleSpanish: La cafetería está al lado del mercado.
vocabulary.exampleEnglish: The café is beside the market.

vocabulary.tip: In Spain, many cafés have outdoor terrace tables.
Use una mesa para dos for a table for two.
vocabulary.exampleSpanish: Una mesa para dos, por favor.

vocabulary.tip: In Spain, la carta is the list of food and drinks.
Use camarera for a woman.
Camarera is common and neutral in Spain.
Un café in Spain often means a small espresso.
A very common breakfast or mid-morning order.
vocabulary.exampleSpanish: Para mí, un café con leche.
vocabulary.exampleEnglish: For me, a coffee with milk.

vocabulary.tip: The accent distinguishes té from te.
In much of Latin America you may hear jugo; this Madrid scene uses zumo.
vocabulary.exampleEnglish: I would like an orange juice.

vocabulary.tip: Agua is feminine but uses el in the singular.
A common simple Spanish breakfast.
The word is familiar; focus on using it in the order.
A bocadillo is a common Spanish sandwich.
In this Madrid café, tortilla means tortilla española.
Often enjoyed with coffee or thick hot chocolate.
Learn quisiera as one polite ordering chunk.
vocabulary.exampleEnglish: I would like a tea, please.

vocabulary.tip: Use it when deliberately choosing between options.
Useful when several people are ordering.
In Spain, tomar is very common for having food or drink.
vocabulary.exampleEnglish: What would you like to have?

vocabulary.tip: Use familiar chunks such as con leche.
Use familiar chunks such as sin azúcar.
Keep sin azúcar together as one useful café chunk.
This returns naturally from the market lesson.
Listen for this after the main order.
Add gracias for a warm close.
La cuenta, por favor is enough.

grammar.meaning: Use one reliable phrase to order politely without learning a full verb system.
grammar.explanation: Use quisiera + item + por favor for a polite café order. Learn it as one complete phrase for now.

notes.title: One simple change
notes.text: Keep con leche and sin azúcar as useful fixed chunks after the item.

notes.title: No conjugation table yet
notes.text: The goal is a successful order, not analyzing every form of querer.

examples.spanish: Quisiera un café con leche, por favor.
examples.english: I would like a coffee with milk, please.

examples.spanish: Quisiera un té sin azúcar, por favor.
examples.english: I would like a tea without sugar, please.

examples.spanish: Quisiera un zumo de naranja pequeño.
examples.english: I would like a small orange juice.

examples.spanish: Para mí, una tostada, por favor.

grammar.practicePrompt: Choose one real drink and order it with quisiera.

dialogue.title: A quiet table after the market
dialogue.setting: Carlos and {{learnerName}} sit on the terrace. The café server comes to take the order.

languageNote.title: One server, two listeners
languageNote.text: The server says ¿Qué vais a tomar? because she is speaking to Carlos and you together. Use ¿Qué quieres tomar? when speaking to one person.

lines.speaker: Carlos
lines.spanish: Qué tranquilo está aquí después del mercado.
lines.english: It’s so peaceful here after the market.

lines.speaker: Carlos
lines.english: What do you prefer, coffee, tea, or juice?

lines.speaker: Learner
lines.intent: Choose coffee, tea, or orange juice.

lines.speaker: Camarera
lines.english: Good morning. What would you like?

lines.speaker: Learner
lines.exampleSpanish: Quisiera un café con leche, por favor.
lines.exampleEnglish: I would like a coffee with milk, please.

lines.speaker: Learner
lines.spanish: Para mí, una tostada, por favor.

lines.speaker: Learner
lines.scene: A little later, after you finish...

comprehension.prompt: What snack does the model conversation include?
How do you end the order?

listening.title: A real café exchange
listening.instructions: Listen, follow along, then check what you understood.
listening.naturalScript: Buenos días. ¿Qué vais a tomar? — Quisiera un café con leche, por favor. — Muy bien. ¿Algo para comer? — Para mí, una tostada. — ¿Algo más? — Nada más, gracias. — La cuenta, por favor.
listening.slowScript: Buenos días. / ¿Qué vais a tomar? / Quisiera un café con leche, / por favor. / Muy bien. / ¿Algo para comer? / Para mí, / una tostada. / ¿Algo más? / Nada más, / gracias. / La cuenta, / por favor.

transcript.speaker: Camarera
transcript.english: Good morning. What would you like?

transcript.speaker: Learner
transcript.spanish: Quisiera un café con leche, por favor.
transcript.english: I would like a coffee with milk, please.

comprehension.prompt: What drink do you order in the model conversation?
comprehension.explanation: Un café con leche is a coffee with milk.

comprehension.prompt: What food do you request in the model conversation?
comprehension.explanation: Para mí, una tostada means toast for me.

comprehension.prompt: How do you say the order is complete?
comprehension.explanation: Nada más, gracias politely says nothing else, thank you.

comprehension.prompt: What do you ask for at the end?
comprehension.explanation: La cuenta, por favor asks for the bill.

options: I would like a coffee with milk.
items.answer: I would like a coffee with milk.

tips: Keep quisiera flowing as one word: kee-SYE-ra.
Stress the final syllable in café.
Link con leche smoothly without rushing either word.
Let por favor sound warm rather than overly formal.

items.note: Keep café clearly stressed at the end.
Pause slightly after té if helpful.
Stress mí because it carries the personal choice.
Use a natural rising question melody.
Keep the whole phrase relaxed and clear.

speaking.instructions: Carlos guides you through the café order one response at a time.

items.label: Listen
items.cue: Hear the polite order once, then say it back.
items.meaning: I would like a coffee, please.

items.label: Your turn
items.cue: Make one simple change to the drink.

items.label: Keep talking
items.cue: Choose your drink and order it politely before opening the model.
items.text: Quisiera un té sin azúcar, por favor.
items.meaning: I would like a tea without sugar, please.
items.focus: Keep the order in one polite sentence.

items.label: One more challenge
items.cue: Close the order warmly and confidently.
items.prompt: Tell the server you need nothing else.

items.label: Finish the conversation
items.cue: State your real preference, then order that drink with one change.
items.prompt: Answer with the drink you chose.
items.text: Prefiero café. Quisiera un café con leche, por favor.
items.meaning: I prefer coffee. I would like a coffee with milk, please.

flashcards.selectionRule: Only Tier 1 café language and mission phrases appear. The learner’s stated drink preference appears first.

coffee: Quisiera un café con leche, por favor.
tea: Quisiera un té sin azúcar, por favor.
juice: Quisiera un zumo de naranja pequeño, por favor.

quiz.prompt: What does quisiera mean in a café order?
quiz.explanation: Quisiera is the lesson’s polite ordering chunk: I would like.

quiz.prompt: Carlos asks ¿Qué prefieres? What is he asking?
quiz.explanation: Prefieres asks which option someone prefers.
El té means tea; the written accent is important.

quiz.prompt: Which phrase means coffee with milk?
quiz.explanation: Con means with, so café con leche is coffee with milk.

quiz.prompt: Which item is something to eat?
quiz.explanation: La tostada means toast and is a common café snack.
Quisiera directly introduces the item you would like to order.

quiz.prompt: Complete: Un té _____ azúcar, por favor.
The server asks ¿Algo más? You are finished. What should you say?
quiz.explanation: This says you need nothing else and closes the order politely.

quiz.prompt: You genuinely prefer orange juice. Which answer fits?
quiz.explanation: Prefiero directly states your real preference.

quiz.prompt: Carlos and you are both ordering. How can you introduce your own snack?
quiz.explanation: Para mí is a natural way to introduce your part of a shared order.

quiz.prompt: Translate: Quisiera un té sin azúcar, por favor.
options: I would like a tea without sugar, please.
quiz.answer: I would like a tea without sugar, please.
quiz.explanation: Quisiera is I would like, and sin azúcar is without sugar.

quiz.prompt: Which phrase asks for the bill?
quiz.explanation: La cuenta is the bill; la carta is the menu.

quiz.prompt: Choose the complete, polite café order.
options: Buenos días. Quisiera un café con leche y una tostada, por favor.
Estoy un café a las diez.
quiz.answer: Buenos días. Quisiera un café con leche y una tostada, por favor.
quiz.explanation: It opens warmly, uses quisiera, names the order, and includes por favor.

quiz.prompt: The server says ¿Qué vais a tomar? What should happen next?
options: Say what you would like to drink.
quiz.answer: Say what you would like to drink.
quiz.explanation: The server is asking what the group will have to drink.

quiz.prompt: Which sequence completes the café mission naturally?
options: Greet → order → make one change → answer ¿Algo más? → ask for the bill
Ask for bill → introduce yourself → choose a market item
Order → leave → greet → ask the time
quiz.answer: Greet → order → make one change → answer ¿Algo más? → ask for the bill
quiz.explanation: That sequence follows the real café exchange from arrival to departure.

quiz.prompt: You want coffee but no sugar. Which order communicates both ideas?
options: Quisiera un café sin azúcar, por favor.
quiz.answer: Quisiera un café sin azúcar, por favor.
quiz.explanation: Place the fixed change sin azúcar after the drink.

quiz.prompt: Carlos asks your preference before the server arrives. Why does that matter?
options: Your order reflects a choice you deliberately made.
Carlos needs to choose the correct grammar answer.
The café only serves one drink.
The lesson requires coffee for everyone.
quiz.answer: Your order reflects a choice you deliberately made.
quiz.explanation: The preference is meaningful because Carlos asked and you explicitly answered.

quiz.prompt: The server asks ¿Qué vais a tomar? Complete the full mission.
options: Quisiera un zumo de naranja pequeño y una tostada, por favor. Nada más, gracias.
Estoy libre el sábado a las diez.
quiz.answer: Quisiera un zumo de naranja pequeño y una tostada, por favor. Nada más, gracias.
quiz.explanation: You order a chosen drink and snack, make a simple size choice, and close politely.

miniConversation.title: Your café order
miniConversation.setting: The server is ready to take your order while Carlos listens without speaking for you.
miniConversation.goal: State your preference, order one drink and snack, make one change, and close politely.

turns.speaker: Carlos
turns.english: What do you prefer to drink?

turns.speaker: Camarera
turns.english: Good morning. What would you like to drink?

turns.speaker: Learner
turns.intent: Order the chosen drink politely and add one change.
turns.exampleSpanish: Quisiera un café con leche, por favor.
turns.exampleEnglish: I would like a coffee with milk, please.

turns.speaker: Learner
turns.spanish: Para mí, una tostada, por favor.

turns.speaker: Carlos
turns.spanish: ¡Excelente! Ya sabes pedir en una cafetería de Madrid.
turns.english: Excellent! Now you know how to order in a Madrid café.

culture.title: A Madrid coffee break
culture.text: At many cafés in Madrid, you can order at the bar or sit at a table. Terrace service may cost a little more. A small espresso is often simply un café, while café con leche contains much more milk. Begin with buenos días, order clearly, and finish with gracias.

regionalNotes.label: Spain
regionalNotes.text: In Madrid and across Spain, zumo is the usual word for juice.

regionalNotes.label: Much of Latin America
regionalNotes.text: Across much of Latin America, you’ll usually hear jugo instead. Both are correct regional choices.

worldBuilding.carlosSpanish: Me gusta esta terraza porque se oye la ciudad, pero aquí estamos tranquilos.
worldBuilding.carlosEnglish: I like this terrace because you can hear the city, but we’re peaceful here.

worldBuilding.carlosSpanish: La camarera ya sabe que normalmente pido café con leche.
worldBuilding.carlosEnglish: The server already knows that I normally order coffee with milk.

worldBuilding.carlosSpanish: Desde aquí podemos caminar hasta una plaza muy bonita.
worldBuilding.carlosEnglish: From here we can walk to a beautiful plaza.

livingWorldInteractions.title: The old espresso machine
livingWorldInteractions.prompt: The machine behind the bar hisses as the server prepares another order. Listen for a moment.
livingWorldInteractions.carlosSpanish: Esta máquina lleva muchos años aquí. La camarera dice que conoce el café perfecto para cada cliente.
livingWorldInteractions.carlosEnglish: This machine has been here for many years. The server says she knows the perfect coffee for every customer.

nativeSpeech.meaning: What are you all going to have?
nativeSpeech.note: Natural in Spain when a server addresses Carlos and you together. Produce ¿Qué quieres tomar? for one person if that feels easier.

nativeSpeech.meaning: Can you get me a coffee?
nativeSpeech.note: Common and natural in Spain. Habla teaches quisiera first because it travels well across regions and situations.

nativeSpeech.meaning: espresso with a small amount of milk
nativeSpeech.note: Recognize it on a Madrid menu; it is optional vocabulary, not part of today’s mission.

commonMistakes.why: Use un with the drink, then keep con leche as one familiar chunk.
Use prefiero to state your drink preference; soy describes identity.
Name the drink first, then add the simple change.

commonMistakes.mistake: La carta, por favor when you want to pay.
commonMistakes.why: La carta is the menu; la cuenta is the bill.

realLifeMission.title: Your Café Order
realLifeMission.presentationBody: Watch the café exchange once, then complete it with the drink you chose.
realLifeMission.mission: Complete one café visit while Carlos lets you speak for yourself. Order the drink you genuinely chose, add a snack and one simple change, respond to the server, and ask for the bill.
successCriteria: Use quisiera for the main order.
Add one drink change or size choice.
Respond to ¿Algo más? and close politely.
realLifeMission.completionResponse: ¡Lo hiciste! You chose what you actually wanted and completed a relaxed Madrid café order without me speaking for you.
realLifeMission.successHeadline: You ordered your own drink in Madrid.
realLifeMission.successCarlos: See? I didn’t need to order for you this time.
realLifeMission.correctionRule: Celebrate the successful order first. Correct only one error that changes the requested item or makes the server misunderstand the change.
realLifeMission.handoff: Después del café, quiero enseñarte una plaza muy bonita. Podemos ir caminando.
realLifeMission.livePracticeCopy: Ready to order again? I’ll be right beside you if you get stuck.

canDo: I can state which drink I prefer.
I can order a drink and snack politely.
I can make one simple change.
I can close the order and ask for the bill.
selfCheck: Can I order without reading the full model?
Can I understand ¿Algo más? at natural speed?
Can I say my real drink preference?
Can I ask for the bill naturally?

passportStamp.title: Café Regular
passportStamp.description: You stated your preference and completed a relaxed café order in Spanish.
passportStamp.unlockCondition: Complete the café mission for Lesson 6.

achievement.title: First Order
achievement.description: Order a drink and snack and make one polite change entirely in Spanish.

carlosClosing: You chose what you really wanted and ordered it without me speaking for you. Enjoy your drink—when we finish, you’ll guide us toward one of my favorite plazas.

carlosClosingByChoice.coffee: You chose coffee and completed your first Madrid café visit without me speaking for you. Enjoy it—after this, you’ll guide us toward one of my favorite plazas.
carlosClosingByChoice.tea: You chose tea and completed your first Madrid café visit without me speaking for you. Enjoy it—after this, you’ll guide us toward one of my favorite plazas.
carlosClosingByChoice.juice: You chose orange juice and completed your first Madrid café visit without me speaking for you. Enjoy it—after this, you’ll guide us toward one of my favorite plazas.
